smartlead_campaign: OR #4 | Ecom Leads | Scroll Stopper | NO Personlization

magnet.report_label: Scroll-Stopper Sheet (the 3 ads we wrote them)

thread.subject: something for H2Ocean
thread.body_text:
hi Edward, just went through your site, not sure how H2Ocean isn't already on my client list haha.

that's not really why I'm here though. I do Meta ads for ecom brands, and rather than pitch you some BS, I just went ahead and built you a few.

built three ad mockups from H2Ocean's actual products, plus a word-class brand playbook I put together from your site and reviews. it's finished and ready to go on my end.

want me to send it your way?

- Kyle

Kyle 

 Sure send me what you are presenting for Meta Ads, we are interested in the creative content.
 

 Thanks
 Eddie

here you go Edward - 2 ad mockups built from H2Ocean's own products, plus your brand playbook. 

 the mockups show the angles, the playbook is the part most brands actually keep - your voice, the words your customers use, and what you can and can't say in ad copy.

 if any of them click, the quickest way to see how I'd actually run them is a 30 min call. → Calendar 

 - Kyle

thread.subject: Re: something for H2Ocean
thread.body_text:
Just set us up for tomorrow. 

 Thanks
 Eddie

copy.tldr: H2Ocean is a 20-year-old natural sea salt mouthwash brand with real dental credibility but zero ads running, no visible reviews, and a low $11 to $17 price - so the whole call is about starting from scratch with the healing angle, not fixing a broken account.

deep_dive.what_they_are: A natural oral care brand out of Stuart, Florida that has been selling a patented sea salt mouthwash for over 20 years. They sell direct on their own site and also talk to dental professionals.
deep_dive.what_they_sell: Two mouthwash products: Healing Rinse Mouthwash in Arctic Ocean Mint and Healing Rinse Mouthwash in Fresh Lemon Ice. Both use Red Sea salt, a natural enzyme, and xylitol. They come in 8oz, 16oz, and 64oz sizes. Site prices show $17 for the Mint and $11 for the Lemon.
deep_dive.positioning: A premium, natural, non-burning alternative to harsh alcohol-based mouthwashes. The original patented sea salt rinse, built for healing after dental work, piercings, and oral irritation, and safe for the whole family.
deep_dive.who_buys: Health-conscious adults who want a gentle rinse with no alcohol, fluoride, or artificial stuff. Big use cases are recovering from dental work, healing oral piercings, soothing canker sores, dry mouth, and chemo mouth. It is also sold as a daily-use family product.
customer_voice: This is great for post-dental work and for oral piercings! It was the only thing that helped my tongue piercing heal. Two thumbs up
strengths: Real credibility - over 20 years in market, a patented formula, clinical studies, and dental professional backing. That is rare and it makes ads more believable.
Clear, specific pain points to target - piercings, post-dental recovery, canker sores, dry mouth. These are things people actively look for relief from, which makes for strong ad angles.
gaps: No reviews show on the site and no ads are running. There is almost no public social proof to lean on, which is the first thing that makes cold traffic hesitate.
Low price point. At $11 to $17 a bottle, a single sale barely covers the cost to get a new customer with paid ads. The math only works with bigger cart sizes or repeat buyers.

game_plan.headline: Start from zero with the piercing and post-dental healing angle, prove one ad can sell, then widen to daily use and add the second flavor.

phases.goal: Prove that a single ad can bring in sales at a price that works. We are not scaling. We are finding one winning ad and one winning audience.
campaigns: One testing campaign with the Scroll-Stopper ads, sending traffic to the Arctic Ocean Mint product page.
A simple retargeting campaign so people who visit but do not buy see the ad again.
lead_products: Healing Rinse Mouthwash - Arctic Ocean Mint. We lead with this one because it is in stock, mint is the safe mainstream flavor most people expect from a mouthwash, and the Lemon Ice is currently marked sold out so we cannot send paid traffic to it.
phases.creative: Go live first with the Scroll-Stopper ad built on the piercing and post-dental healing angle. That is the strongest one because it uses the brand's own best line - 'the only thing that helped my tongue piercing heal' - and it targets a clear, urgent problem where people are desperate for relief. We test the other Scroll-Stopper ads against it. We need to confirm with Eddie he is okay showing the real customer quote in ads.
phases.budget: Around $75 to $100 a day to start.
phases.what_success_looks_like: We can see which ad and which audience bring the cheapest sales, and at least one combination is selling at a cost we would be happy to put more money behind. If nothing sells, we learn why before spending more.

phases.goal: Put more money behind whatever won in month one and widen who we show it to.
campaigns: Scale the winning ad and audience with a bigger daily budget.
Add a new audience angle around daily family oral care and dry mouth to reach beyond the recovery crowd.
Keep retargeting running and add a small offer to lift cart size, like a bundle or a bigger bottle.
lead_products: Healing Rinse Mouthwash - Arctic Ocean Mint stays the lead. If Lemon Ice is back in stock by now, we test it as a second angle for people who dislike mint.
phases.creative: Refresh the winning ad so it does not wear out. Build two or three new versions of the same winning angle. If Eddie can get us even a few real customer reviews or a short video, we work those in, because social proof is the biggest thing missing.
phases.budget: Step up gradually toward $150 to $200 a day, only on what is already working.
phases.what_success_looks_like: Sales are steady and predictable at a cost that leaves room for profit, and the bigger bottle or bundle is lifting the average order size so the math works better.

phases.goal: Scale the winner and bring the second product in properly.
campaigns: Scale the proven ads and audiences further.
Launch a dedicated campaign for the second flavor, Fresh Lemon Ice, assuming it is back in stock.
Push repeat purchase - email signups and reminders to reorder, since mouthwash runs out and this is a refill product.
lead_products: Healing Rinse Mouthwash - Fresh Lemon Ice added as its own campaign for the zesty-flavor crowd, alongside the Arctic Ocean Mint that carried the first two months.
phases.creative: Run the proven angles at higher spend plus fresh versions to keep them from getting stale. Add a Lemon Ice specific ad for people who want a lemon taste instead of mint.
phases.budget: Scale in steps based on results. No fixed ceiling - we only spend more where sales hold.
phases.what_success_looks_like: Two products selling through ads, a repeat-buyer habit forming, and a clear, repeatable way to spend more and get more back.

estimates.label: Starting budget
estimates.basis: Enough to get roughly 30 to 50 clicks a day at typical costs in this category, which is the floor for learning which ad and audience actually sell before we spend more.

estimates.label: Assumed average order value
estimates.value: About $11 per order as a stand-in
estimates.basis: We used the median of their two listed prices ($11 and $17) as a stand-in for average order value since we cannot see their real cart data. Their real number is likely higher because of 16oz and 64oz sizes and multi-bottle orders - we need Eddie to confirm.

estimates.label: Revenue we would aim to add
estimates.value: We are not putting a dollar figure on this yet
estimates.basis: With no ad account access and an order value this low, any 90-day revenue number would be a guess. Once we know the real average order size and their repeat purchase rate, we can set an honest target. That is why those are the first questions on the call.

assumptions: Arctic Ocean Mint is in stock and can take paid traffic. Lemon Ice is currently sold out, so month one leans on Mint only.
Eddie is okay using the real customer quote about the tongue piercing in ad copy.

The real average order value is higher than $11 once bigger bottles and multi-bottle carts are counted. If it truly sits at $11 with no repeat buying, the whole plan needs a bundle or subscription to survive.
The checkout works and the site can convert traffic. We have not been able to test a purchase.
There is a real budget to test with for at least 30 days. Paid ads need runway to learn.

questions.question: Are you running any paid ads right now, on Meta or anywhere else?
questions.listening_for: We found no Meta ads live. Confirm if they truly do zero paid, or stopped, or run somewhere else. If they stopped, ask why - that tells us what already failed.

questions.question: Who handles marketing today - you, someone in-house, or another agency?
questions.listening_for: Whether there is anyone to work with, and whether we would be replacing someone. If a past agency failed, find out what they tried.

questions.question: When you have tried ads before, what happened?
questions.listening_for: Did ads ever run and lose money, or has this never been tried at all? This changes whether we are fixing or starting fresh.

questions.question: At $11 to $17 a bottle, what does a typical order actually look like - do people buy one bottle, or bigger sizes and multiples?
questions.listening_for: This is the most important question. If most orders are one small bottle, ads will struggle to pay off. If people buy 64oz or several bottles, the math works. We need their real average order size.

questions.question: Roughly what does it cost you to make and ship a bottle, so we know how much room there is to spend to get a customer?
questions.listening_for: Their margin. A cheap product with thin margin cannot carry paid ads. We need to know how much we can afford to spend per sale.

questions.question: Do customers come back and reorder, and if so how often?
questions.listening_for: Mouthwash runs out, so repeat buying could be strong. If a customer is worth several orders a year, we can afford to spend more to get them the first time. This can save the whole plan.

questions.question: When is Fresh Lemon Ice coming back in stock?
questions.listening_for: It shows sold out. If it is coming back soon, we can plan for it in phase three. If it is discontinued, we lean on Mint only. Do not build ads to a dead product.

questions.question: You have over a million happy customers and 20 years in market - why are there no reviews showing on the site?
questions.listening_for: This is the biggest gap for ads. Cold buyers need proof. Find out if reviews exist somewhere we can pull in, or if they can gather some. No proof makes ads much harder.

questions.question: Out of all the reasons people buy - piercings, post-dental, canker sores, dry mouth, daily use - which group actually buys the most?
questions.listening_for: Confirms which angle to lead with. We are betting on piercing and post-dental healing. If daily family use is actually their biggest group, we adjust the first ad.

questions.question: When someone lands on your product page, do they usually buy, or do a lot of them leave?
questions.listening_for: Whether the problem is getting people there or converting them once they arrive. If the page does not convert, ads alone will not fix it and we should say so.

questions.question: Are there any claims your legal or compliance side will not let you make in ads?
questions.listening_for: We already know we cannot say cures, fixes, prevents cancer, or treats chemo side effects, and we must avoid loose eco claims. Confirm their own limits so an ad does not get rejected or cause trouble.

questions.question: Do you know if people search Google for things like sea salt mouthwash or how to heal a tongue piercing?
questions.listening_for: There is a real opening here. People with piercings and post-dental pain actively search for relief. If they are not showing up in search, Google ads and SEO are a genuine second step, not a forced upsell.

questions.question: You have a big Knowledge Center with articles and clinical studies - is that bringing you any traffic from Google today?
questions.listening_for: They already have content built. If it is not ranking or driving sales, there is a clear SEO opening to make that existing work pay off. Only push this if they are curious.

objections.objection: We have never run ads and are not sure they work for us.
objections.answer: That is exactly why we start small. Month one is not about scale, it is about proving one ad can sell at a cost you are happy with, on a modest budget. If it does not work, you learn why before spending big. You have a rare advantage most brands do not - 20 years, a patent, and dental backing - so there is real trust to build ads on.

objections.objection: Our mouthwash is only $11 to $17. Can ads even make money at that price?
objections.answer: Fair concern, and I am watching it closely. At one small bottle per order, it is tight. But two things change the math - your bigger 16oz and 64oz sizes, and the fact that mouthwash runs out so people reorder. If your real average order and repeat buying are what I expect, ads work. That is why I want your real order size before I promise any number.

objections.objection: We do not really have reviews showing on the site.
objections.answer: I noticed that, and it is the first thing I would want to fix. Cold buyers need proof before they trust a new brand. You say you have over a million happy customers, so the proof exists - we just need to surface even a handful in the ads and on the page. That alone can lift results a lot.

objections.objection: We only care about the creative right now, not a full service.
objections.answer: Understood, and the creative is the piece most brands keep from what I sent. But the ad is only half of it. Same ad can win or lose depending on who sees it and where it sends them. The 90-day plan is how those ads actually turn into sales. Happy to start where you are comfortable and grow from there.

red_flags.flag: Low price point of $11 to $17 per bottle.
red_flags.why_it_matters: A single sale barely covers the cost of getting a new customer with paid ads. The plan only survives if the real average order is bigger or customers reorder. Do not promise results before confirming this.

red_flags.flag: No reviews visible anywhere on the site.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Cold traffic needs proof to trust a brand it has never seen. With no social proof to show, ads will convert worse and cost more until we can surface real reviews.

red_flags.flag: Fresh Lemon Ice is marked sold out.
red_flags.why_it_matters: We cannot send paid traffic to a product people cannot buy. Month one must lead with Arctic Ocean Mint only, and the second-product phase depends on Lemon Ice coming back.

red_flags.flag: Health and healing claims in a regulated space.
red_flags.why_it_matters: Meta restricts health claims and the brand cannot legally say cures, fixes, prevents cancer, or treats chemo side effects. Ads must be worded carefully or they get rejected or cause bigger problems.

red_flags.flag: We have never seen a purchase go through their checkout.
red_flags.why_it_matters: If checkout is broken or clunky, we would be paying to send people to a page that cannot sell. Worth confirming it works before spending.

other_channel_openings: Google Search ads for high-intent terms like sea salt mouthwash, tongue piercing aftercare, and canker sore relief. People with these problems actively search for a fix, so this is a real second step once Meta is proven.

SEO on their existing Knowledge Center. They already have articles and clinical studies built. If that content is not ranking, there is honest room to make it pull in free search traffic that converts.
